title: 第 36 節
en: Another perplexing name is Mamurius, which is evidently derived from the same root mar, but in the sense of dying, and has, I believe, nothing whatever to do with Mars. The idea that this Mamurius is Mars has created great confusion. Mamurius has, I believe, no more to do with Mars than that in the month of Mars when the new year begins the old dying year Veturius Mamurius dies and has to be buried.

The birth of the new year and the death of the old were still celebrated in Germany when I was a boy. They probably continue to be celebrated in outlying villages even now, though those who celebrate them are hardly aware whether these festivities are of Christian or pre-Christian origin. But while such customs are rapidly dying out in the towns of Germany before the approach of elementary schools, railways, and telegraphs, they seem to continue to form the chief staple of public festivities among many of the Slavonic tribes in the east' of Europe. To them the birth of the vernal sun, his fight against the army of winter, his final victory, his summer vi] MAMURIUS. 715 sway, his autumnal decline, and at last his death, are the great events of the year ; and even when these popular rejoicings and mournings have been changed into Christian festivals, the old heathen features can often be recognised behind their new ecclesiastical veil1.

To us the expression that ' the year has died ' is common enough, but when using that phrase we hardly think of more than that the year is gone. Our year dies with the civil year on the last day of December, Sylvester-day, and the burying of St. Sylvester 2 on that day, together with many other not quite Christian customs, prove that this period was once observed as a pagan festival. In many places it is neither Christmas, nor the Sylvesternight, but the Epiphany which is considered as the real beginning of the Christian new year. But with the ancient people the fight of the sun against the old year, or against the might of winter continued till the first signs of spring, of new light and new warmth appeared. The ancient Romans began their year with the month of March, the Slaves with the return of spring. At that time, about the vernal equinox, it is still the custom in many parts of Europe to ' carry out the year,' that is, to bury it. To carry out was meant originally for carrying the dead body out of the village to burn or bury it, the Latin efferre, or condere 3. In the Voigtland (in Saxony) the children were seen some thirty years ago, and possibly may still be 1 See Hanusch, Wissenschaft der Slavischen Mythen, p. 140 ; Grimm, Deutsche Mythologie, p. 730; Usener, Italische Mythen, p. 189.
VOL. II. U 716 MAMURIUS. [chap.
seen on the Sunday of Laetare (March), marching through the streets and singing —
'Wir alle, wir alle kommen 'raus, Und tragen heut den Tod 'naus.
Komm, Friihling, wieder mit uns in das Dorf, Willkommen, lieber Friihling.' 'We all, we all are coming forth, And carry out Death to-day.
Come, Spring, with us again into the village, Welcome, dear Spring.'
Here Death is clearly meant for Winter, the death of nature, and opposed to Spring, as the new year and the new life.

These customs and traditions change according as Winter or the dying year is conceived as an old man or as an old woman. Their time also varies slightly according as the seasons vary, and as the real periods of the vernal and autumnal equinox are modified by civil or ecclesiastical holidays. Besides the days already mentioned, the Sunday of Laetare (Mid -Lent), Good Friday, the day of St. Gregory, the day of St. Rupertus (27 March), or the Monday after the vernal equinox, the first of April, even the first of May, have all been chosen in different parts of Europe for ' carrying out Death or Winter.' But the four great events in the annual career of the sun remained everywhere the four great events in the life of a family or of a village, and produced sayings, riddles, and legends which still pervade, though hardly understood, the phraseology of our modern life.

I shall give a few extracts from the collections made by Hanusch, Grimm, and Usener (Italische Mythen), and though the repetition of the same Vl] MAMURIUS. 717 stories may seem tedious, it is after all only by an accumulation of evidence that the incredulity of those who doubt the existence of solar customs and solar myths and of all that is connected with solar worship, can be effectually removed.

The Slaves, wherever we meet them in their village life, tell of an old woman who about the time of the vernal equinox (sometimes on the Sunday of Laetare, sometimes on Palm-Sunday) is carried out and either burnt, or buried, or drowned, or cut, or sawed to pieces. In Moravia she is called Marena, in Poland and Silesia Marzana, in Bohemia Smrt, among the Wends Smerc, elsewhere Muriena or Mamurienda (cf. Mamurius).

All these words seem to have meant originally Death and Winter, while the new year which is sometimes brought into the village when the old year has been carried out, is in Bohemian songs called Nove leto l, the new summer.

The Masures, another Slavonic tribe, relate that on the 12th of March, the day of St. Gregory, Zima (the winter) goes to sea. Generally the Winter or the Old Year is represented by a figure made of straw, and is rendered hideous by a white mask and other paraphernalia. Sometimes it is not considered enough either to burn or to drown that figure, but it is beaten, dragged about, nailed against a tree, and finally burnt, while the ashes are scattered over the fields or thrown into the water. Among both northern and southern Slaves 2 1 ' The Slavonic leto corresponds to our Lent ; in Bohemia it is used for year; while jaro, the German year, means in Bohemian spring.' Usener, 1. c, p. 194.
2 Usener, p. 191.
U 2 718 MAMURIUS. [chap.

this Marena is sawed in two, and the children are made to believe that an old woman has actually been taken out of the village and killed with a saw. Exactly the same custom and the same belief, what the Slaves call babu fezati, ' to saw the old woman,' must have existed among the Romanic nations also, for segare la vecchia, ' to saw the old woman,' is the name given to a popular ' amusement at Barcelona, where on the same Sunday of Laetare boys may be seen running through the streets, carrying saws, and asking for the oldest woman that they may carry her out of the town and saw her in pieces.

In Italy, again, at the same time, a hideous doll is taken through the streets, and under fearful shoutings sawed in two. In Venice this custom is called siegar la vechia, near Eoveredo and Triest segar la veccia, in Tuscany segare la monaca 2. These are striking and startling coincidences, more so than the mere burning or drowning of the figure would have been. In Parma a doll that is burnt on MidLent is called la veccia da brusar, while in Tuscany the usual expression is fare il giorgio 3.
While these popular amusements embody the recollection of the death of the old year about the time of the vernal solstice, similar customs 1 Grimm, Deutsche Mythologie, p. 742.

2 P. Fanfani, Vocabolario dell' uso toscano, p. 805, s. v. scampanata ; Boerio, Dizionario del dialetto veneziano, p. 660 ; Giamb. Azzolini, Vocabolario vernacolo-italiano, p. 408 ; G. Patriarchi, Vocabolario veneziano e padovano, p. 179 ; A. Eobiola, Dizion. univers. della lingua ital. (1835), 4, 429 ; quoted by Usener, 1. c, p. 192.
s Usener explains this expression as borrowed from St. George (April 23), who killed the dragon and delivered the maid, possibly (horribile dictu) again a solar myth or custom.

vi] MAMURIUS. 719 have gathered round the Feast of the Epiphany, the first manifestation of Christ to the Gentiles. The doll which is carried about and destroyed at that time is called the Befana or Befania, which is accepted as a corruption of Epiphania. This certainly sounds strange, because it seems that the Epiphany itself would more naturally have been identified with customs celebrating the return of light, and of the new year, whether natural or civil, and not with the expulsion of the old year. This requires some explanation. But the two events hang together, and the fact remains that on the day of the Epiphany or on the day before it, the same custom with outbursts of popular hilarity can be witnessed in Italy and in Switzerland, a figure representing some detested or dangerous character being carried about under hideous noises, and finally disposed of. Different from this is the carrying off or burying of the Carnival, which seems to have little to do with any prominent epoch of the natural year, and to signify no more than a bidding farewell to the amusements of the season \ If the burying of the Carnival is called in some places bruciare la vecchia, — and this is doubtful, — it could only be so because the original meaning of bruciare la vecchia was completely forgotten.

These extracts may suffice to show how many of our still existing popular customs and festivals owe their origin to the observation of the influence of the sun on nature and on man, and how many stories and legends that are now told of La Vecchia, of Muriena, or Befana of Mamurienda or Mamurius, 1 See, however, Usener, I.e., pp. 199, 202.
had at first a purely solar meaning. In ancient times both customs and legends were naturally far more transparent and intelligible than they are at present.

That the lustrum at Rome represented a quinquennial solar cycle, has never been doubted, but that lustrum facere meant originally a burying of the cycle, as the burying of La Vecchia meant the burying of the old year, is an ingenious guess of Usener, which deserves serious consideration *.

Each year was inaugurated at Rome by the festival of Anna Perenna on the Ides of March. People drank as many draughts of wine as they wished to live years, and the result may not have been very different from what happens in modern Rome on the Sunday of Laetare.

Anna, the year, wrongfully identified with Anna, the sister of Dido, was supposed to have been drowned in the river Numicius, and this seems to have been all that remained of the drowning of the old year 2.

Besides this festival of Anna Perenna at the time of the vernal equinox, there was another, shortly before the time of the summer solstice, dedicated to Anna, and another legend was told of her as an old woman, who had once during a famine fed the plebs with cakes. This may refer to Anna as the goddess of the year, in the sense of harvest, for we have an ancient sacrificial formula, preserved by Varro 3, ' Te 1 Usener, 1. c, pp. 204, 206. Liv. i, 44, ' ibi instructum exercitum omnem suovetaurilibus lustra vifc, idque conditum lustrum appellatum, quia in censendo finis factus est. '
- Corssen, in K. Z., ii, 34 ; Usener, 1. c, p. 208.
3 Varro, Fragm. ed. Buecheler, p. 219 ; Usener, 1. c, p. 209.
zh: 另一個令人困惑的名字是瑪慕流斯（Mamurius），它顯然源自同一個詞根 mar，但在此處取「死亡」之義，我認為它與馬爾斯完全無關。將瑪慕流斯等同於馬爾斯的觀念造成了莫大的混淆。我相信瑪慕流斯與馬爾斯毫無關係，除了在馬爾斯月份（新年開始之時），那個正在衰亡的舊年老瑪慕流斯（Veturius Mamurius）必須死亡並被埋葬這一點外。

新年的誕生與舊年的死亡在我還是男孩時，在德國仍被慶祝著。這些慶典即使在現今仍可能在偏遠的村莊繼續舉行，儘管慶祝它們的人幾乎不清楚這些節慶究竟源自基督教還是前基督教時期。但當這類習俗在德國城鎮面對初等教育、鐵路與電報的來臨而迅速消亡之際，它們似乎仍在東歐許多斯拉夫民族中繼續構成公共節慶的主要內容。對他們而言，春日之陽的誕生、他對抗冬季大軍的戰鬥、他最終的勝利、他的夏日統治、他的秋日衰落，以及最終他的死亡，都是一年中的偉大事件；即使當這些民間的歡樂與哀悼已被改變為基督教節日時，那古老的異教特徵往往仍能在其新的教會外衣後被認出來。

對我們來說，「年已死去」這樣的說法是司空見慣的，但在使用這個短語時，我們幾乎只是想著年份已經過去。我們的年隨著西曆年在十二月最後一天——聖西爾維斯特日（Sylvester-day）——而終結，而聖西爾維斯特在那一天的埋葬儀式，連同許多其他並不完全基督教化的習俗，證明這個時期曾經被視為異教節慶。在許多地方，真正開始的基督教新年既不是聖誕節，也不是聖西爾維斯特之夜，而是主顯節。但對於古代民族而言，太陽與舊年或冬季力量的戰鬥一直持續到春天的最初跡象——新光與新暖——出現為止。古代羅馬人以三月開始他們的年份，斯拉夫民族則以春天的回歸開始。在那個時期，大約在春分時節，在歐洲許多地方仍然有「送出年份」的習俗，也就是說，埋葬它。「送出」原本的意思是把屍體送出村莊以便火化或埋葬，拉丁文為 efferre 或 condere。在福格特蘭德（薩克森地區），大約在三十年前曾看到兒童們進行這樣的活動，現在可能仍然
卷二 U 716 瑪木里烏斯。【章節】
在歡樂主日（Laetare）（三月）看到他們在街道上行進並唱著歌——
我們都，我們都要走出去，今天要把死亡帶出來。 （註：此句為德文詩句，根據規則說明應不翻譯，但考量原文實為德文且無英文版本提供，如需英文翻譯版本才能進行繁體中文翻譯，敬請提供英文原文。）
「來吧，春天，再與我們回到村莊，歡迎，親愛的春天。」「我們都，我們都要出來，今天要送走死亡。」
來吧，春天，與我們再次進入村莊，歡迎，親愛的春天。
此處死亡明確指涉冬季——自然的死亡——與春季相對立，春季乃新年與新生命之象徵。

這些風俗和傳統因冬季或垂死的年份被構想為老人或老婦而變化。這些時間也會根據季節的變化而略有不同，並隨著真正的春分和秋分時期因民間或教會假日的改變而有所調整。除了已經提及的日子之外，中齋節週日（Laetare，四旬齋中期）、耶穌受難日、聖格列高里日、聖魯珀圖斯日（三月二十七日）或春分後的星期一、四月一日，甚至五月一日，都曾在歐洲不同地區被選作「驅逐死亡或冬季」的日期。然而，太陽年度運行中的四個偉大事件在各處都始終是家族或村落生命中的四個偉大事件，並產生了諺語、謎語和傳說，這些至今仍滲透了現代生活的措辭，儘管其含義已鮮少被理解。
我將從漢努施（Hanusch）、格林（Grimm）和烏塞納（Usener）所編纂的文集（《義大利神話》*Italische Mythen*）中引述若干篇章。雖然相同故事的重複可能顯得冗長，但歸根結底，只有通過證據的不斷累積，才能有效地消除那些懷疑太陽習俗、太陽神話及與太陽崇拜相關之一切存在的人們心中的疑慮。

斯拉夫人無論在哪裡的村落生活中出現，都流傳著一個古老的女人的故事。她在春分時節左右（有時在四旬齋主日（Sunday of Laetare），有時在棕枝主日（Palm-Sunday））被抬出來，然後被焚燒、埋葬、溺水、割裂，或者被鋸成碎片。在摩拉維亞，她被稱為瑪麗娜（Marena），在波蘭和西里西亞被稱為馬茲納（Marzana），在波希米亞被稱為史墨特（Smrt），在溫德人中被稱為史梅爾克（Smerc），在其他地方則被稱為慕瑞娜（Muriena）或瑪慕瑞恩達（Mamurienda）（參照瑪慕流斯（Mamurius））。
這些詞彙似乎原本都意指死亡與冬季，而在舊年被送出村莊之後有時被迎入村莊的新年，在波希米亞歌謠中被稱為 Nove leto（新夏季）。

馬蘇雷人（Masures）是另一支斯拉夫部族，他們述說在3月12日聖格列高里日（St. Gregory）這一天，冬季（Zima）進入大海。通常冬季或舊年是由一個稻草製成的人形來代表，並被白色面具和其他裝飾品弄得面目可憎。有時光是焚燒或溺斃那個人形還不夠，它還要被毆打、拖曳、釘在樹上，最後才被焚燒，而灰燼則被撒入田野或拋入水中。在北方和南方斯拉夫人之間皆有此俗 ²¹ ²¹ 斯拉夫語的 *leto* 對應於我們的四旬期；在波希米亞，它被用來表示年份；而日耳曼語的 *year* 在波希米亞語中意為春季。*Usener, 1. c, p. 194.*
2 烏塞納（Usener），第 191 頁。
U 2 718 瑪穆留斯。[章

這個瑪瑞娜被鋸成兩截，孩子們被引導相信一位老婦人確實被帶出村莊並用鋸子殺害了。完全相同的風俗和信念——斯拉夫人稱之為 babu fezati，即「鋸老婦人」——必定也曾存在於羅曼民族之中，因為 segare la vecchia，「鋸老婦人」，是一項流行於巴塞隆納的「娛樂活動」的名稱，在同一個歡樂主日（Laetare）的主日，可以看到男孩們跑過街道，手持鋸子，詢問誰是村中最年長的婦人，以便他們將她帶出城鎮並把她鋸成碎片。

在意大利，同一時期，一個駭人的布娃娃被帶過街道，在可怕的叫喊聲中被鋸成兩半。在威尼斯，這個習俗被稱為 siegar la vechia，在羅韋雷托和的里雅斯特附近稱為 segar la veccia，在托斯卡納稱為 segare la monaca²。這些是驚人且令人詫異的巧合，遠比單純焚燒或溺斃該塑像更為如此。在帕瑪，在中旬四旬節焚燒的布娃娃被稱為 la veccia da brusar，而在托斯卡納通常的說法是 fare il giorgio³。
當這些民間娛樂活動在春分時節左右體現了對舊年死亡的紀念時，類似的風俗習慣 --- **註**：此句似乎未完整，原文在"customs"後有註腳標記但句子未終。如有完整原文，請提供以便提供更完善的翻譯。

2 P. Fanfani, *Vocabolario dell' uso toscano*, p. 805, s. v. scampanata ; Boerio, *Dizionario del dialetto veneziano*, p. 660 ; Giamb. Azzolini, *Vocabolario vernacolo-italiano*, p. 408 ; G. Patriarchi, *Vocabolario veneziano e padovano*, p. 179 ; A. Eobiola, *Dizion. univers. della lingua ital.* (1835), 4, 429 ; 引自 Usener, 1. c, p. 192.
烏塞納解釋此表述係借自聖喬治（四月二十三日），他殺死龍並拯救少女，可能（horribile dictu）又是一個太陽神話或習俗。

vi] 瑪穆里烏斯。719 人們聚集在主顯節（Epiphany）的慶典周圍，此為基督首次向非猶太人顯現之日。在此期間被攜帶並毀滅的娃娃被稱為貝法納（Befana）或貝法尼亞（Befania），被認為是「主顯節」（Epiphania）的訛變。這聽起來確實奇異，因為似乎主顯節本身會更自然地與慶祝光的回歸以及新年的習俗相聯結——無論是自然年或民間年——而不是與舊年的驅逐相聯結。這需要某種解釋。然而這兩個事件相互關聯，事實是在主顯節當日或前一日，在意大利和瑞士可以目睹同樣的習俗，伴隨著民眾歡樂的爆發，代表某個令人厭惡或危險人物的形象被攜帶著，並伴隨著可怕的噪聲，最終被處置。與此不同的是狂歡節的送別或埋葬，似乎與自然年的任何主要時期關係甚微，不過表示與季節娛樂的告別。若狂歡節的埋葬在某些地方被稱為「bruciare la vecchia」——這令人懷疑——則只能是因為「bruciare la vecchia」的原始含義已被完全遺忘。
這些摘錄足以說明我們許多仍然存在的民間習俗和節慶，如何源自於對太陽對自然與人類影響的觀察，以及許多現在講述關於「老婦」（La Vecchia）、穆里埃娜（Muriena）或貝法納（Befana）、馬穆里埃達（Mamurienda）或馬穆流斯（Mamurius）的故事和傳說的起源。1 1 參閱 Usener, I.c., pp. 199, 202.
最初具有純粹的太陽意義。在上古時代，風俗與傳說自然遠比現今更加透明易懂。
羅馬的淨化期（lustrum）代表五年制太陽週期，此點向無疑義。但淨化期儀式（lustrum facere）原本意謂埋葬該週期，如同埋葬《老婦》（La Vecchia）意謂埋葬舊年一般，乃是烏塞納（Usener）的精妙推測，堪值認真考究*。
在羅馬，每一年都以三月十五日（Ides of March）安娜‧佩倫娜（Anna Perenna）的節慶揭開序幕。人們飲用他們希望活著享受的年數相等的杯數葡萄酒，其結果與現代羅馬在喜樂主日（Sunday of Laetare）所發生的情形可能沒有太大區別。
安娜（Anna），即年份，被錯誤地認同為迪多（Dido）的妹妹安娜，據說曾溺斃於努米西烏斯河（Numicius），這似乎就是古老年份溺水這一主題所遺留下來的全部內容¹。

除了春分時舉行的安娜‧佩倫娜（Anna Perenna）節慶外，還有另一個節慶在夏至前不久舉行，同樣獻祭給安娜，並有另一則關於她的傳說——她是一位老婦人，曾在饑荒時期用餅餵養平民。這可能涉及安娜作為年份女神的身份，在收穫的意義上，因為我們有一份由瓦羅保存下來的古代獻祭公式¹， ¹ Usener，同上，第 204、206 頁。Liv. i, 44, 「ibi instructum exercitum omnem suovetaurilibus lustra vic, idque conditum lustrum appellatum, quia in censendo finis factus est.」
科森（Corssen）著，《比較文法期刊》（K. Z.）第二卷，第 34 頁；烏澤納（Usener）著，同上引，第 208 頁。
3 瓦羅《殘篇》，布希勒版，第219頁；烏塞納，同前，第209頁。
